transcript.text: I’m a data analyst with around three years of experience at a mid‑size firm. I took the qualifier test because I was curious whether the IIM Ranchi program could offer any fresh insights or help me progress further in my career.
introduce yourself

I’m a data analyst with about three years of experience in analytics at a mid‑size company. I cleared the qualifier test because I wanted to see if an IIM Ranchi certification could add real value to my current skill set and career growth.
What are you looking for?

I’m looking to deepen my expertise in applying machine‑learning and AI at a strategic level – essentially moving from execution to leadership on analytics projects. I’d like to know how the ML/AI modules are structured for someone already working in the field, and how employers typically view an IIM Ranchi certificate for an experienced analyst.
Thank you.

That makes sense. Could you tell me exactly what topics the “Machine Learning for Business — Foundations” and “Advanced Analytics, AI Applications and Leadership” modules cover? Also, is the program geared more toward beginners, or is there flexibility for professionals like me who already have a solid analytics background?

Yes, this program is designed for all we start from foundations so that even if you are not having that round in that course, you will be able to understand it from the foundations only and then we will go deep into that when you are going through the course.

Got it. Could you break down the specific topics covered in the “Machine Learning for Business — Foundations” module – for example, which algorithms, tools, or case studies are included? And similarly, what does the “Advanced Analytics, AI Applications and Leadership” module focus on – are there leadership frameworks, particular industry use‑cases, or hands‑on projects? Also, do employers generally view this certification as a differentiator for someone already working in analytics?

phaseBreakdown.name: Opening
phaseBreakdown.summary: Opening: Counsellor greeted Prashant by name, acknowledged BCom second year, and pivoted to an open question about how interest in analytics started.
phaseBreakdown.didWell: Used the student's name correctly and confirmed background accurately, avoiding the disjointed level-1 anchor.
phaseBreakdown.toImprove: Missed the chance to hand the wheel to the student ('any queries from your end?') and build a personal beat around Pune, BCom, or his story before pivoting to business.

phaseBreakdown.name: Discovery
phaseBreakdown.summary: Discovery: A single open-ended question was asked about how interest developed; the student revealed he had no real interest and no plan.

phaseBreakdown.toImprove: Should have followed up with goal, family context, expected package, timeline, and what 'curiosity' means to him — the call effectively died at this point.

phaseBreakdown.name: Presentation
phaseBreakdown.summary: Presentation: No programme content was presented at all.

phaseBreakdown.toImprove: Even with a low-intent student, the counsellor should have anchored value with a 30-second curriculum/fee/credential hook (IIM Ranchi, 6 months, ₹70,000) to give Prashant something concrete to react to.

phaseBreakdown.toImprove: Should have validated the honesty, reframed 'curiosity' as a legitimate entry point, and pre-empted parent/fee/trust concerns before they could harden.

phaseBreakdown.name: Close
phaseBreakdown.summary: Close: No payment ask, no follow-up, no next step was offered.
phaseBreakdown.didWell: Did not apply pressure or invent scarcity — credit for not coercing.

drills.title: Mirror-before-question drill

rubric.label: Rapport & Opening
rubric.justification: Matches the 1-anchor: trust was destroyed within the first few minutes — the counsellor opened with the wrong self-introduction ('Hutaifat said'), then escalated to abusive language in turn 15 ('भाई तेरे लिए कोर्स नहीं बना तू चला जा') and turn 19 ('अबे गांड मारा भाग'), making the opening disjointed and hostile rather than a genuine connection.

rubric.label: Needs Discovery
rubric.justification: The counsellor asked exactly one qualifying question ('why you wanted to enroll into this course?') in turn 1 and never probed the analytics-vs-entrepreneurship tension the student repeatedly raised, then told him the course is 'not for you' in turn 17 — closer to the 1-anchor of pitching around the student's stated need than to deep discovery.

rubric.label: Programme Presentation
rubric.justification: Turn 5 listed the seven modules in Hindi in roughly the right order, but Module 03 ('Leveraging AI for Smarter Startups') was described only as a generic tool demo in turn 11 ('all the latest tools that are in the market…handle your data more efficiently'), missing the AI-leverage framing and never tailoring the pitch to Vikram's analytics background — a one-size-fits-all delivery closer to a 2 than a 3.

rubric.label: Objection Handling
rubric.justification: Matches the 1-anchor: when the student said the AI/entrepreneurship fit was wrong, the counsellor steamrolled by telling him to leave ('I think this course is not for you. You can, uh, probably leave this, uh, counseling call') instead of engaging the concern, and when the student asked to continue, the counsellor immediately jumped to a payment ask with no acknowledgment of the fit concern.

rubric.label: Product Knowledge & Accuracy
rubric.justification: The module list in turn 5 was broadly accurate, but the seat-block amount was quoted as 'around 4,000 rupees' in turn 25 when the actual non-refundable seat-block is ₹3,000 — a direct factual contradiction of the ground-truth fee structure, and the AI module was mischaracterised as a tools-only prototyping block.

rubric.label: Closing & Payment Ask
rubric.justification: Matches the 1-anchor: the closing was essentially coercive and confusing — coming right after an apology for abusive language, the counsellor asked for the wrong amount (₹4,000 instead of the actual ₹3,000 seat-block) with no context, no deadline framing, and no walk-through of the payment steps.

rubric.label: Communication & Empathy
rubric.justification: Matches the 1-anchor pattern of talking down — the counsellor used abusive and mocking language ('भाई तेरे लिए कोर्स नहीं बना तू चला जा', 'अबे गांड मारा भाग', 'मेरे को रिस्पॉन्ड कर तू कुछ बावड़ी तरेड'), then laughed it off and pretended a colleague said it, showing zero empathy for the working professional's stated upskilling need.

drills.title: Recovery from an emotional rupture mid-call
drills.instruction: Pair with a coach who will deliberately insult or mock you on a practice call. Your job is to (1) pause, (2) name what just happened ('I think that came across as disrespectful and I'm sorry'), (3) ask the student how they'd like to proceed, and (4) only resume selling after explicit re-opt-in. Practice until the recovery feels natural under 15 seconds.

drills.title: Reframe a fit-mismatch objection instead of dismissing it
drills.instruction: Record 5 mock calls where the student is a working professional in analytics/data who says 'this is not for me, I need AI upskilling'. Review the recordings and tag every dismissal phrase you catch.
